---
title: "Data"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Load data
r8_data    <- read_excel("case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r8-2023-2024.xlsx",
                    col_names = FALSE)[-c(1,2), ]


# Nonfatal rates from R8 
names(r8_data)[1]  <- "industry"
names(r8_data)[2]  <- "naics"
names(r8_data)[3]  <- "case_type"
names(r8_data)[4]  <- "total_rate"
names(r8_data)[10] <- "fall_rate"

mfg <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW")
fin <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "52",    case_type == "DAFW")
pro <- r8_data |> filter(naics == "54",    case_type == "DAFW")

mfg_total <- as.numeric(mfg$total_rate[1])
mfg_falls <- as.numeric(mfg$fall_rate[1])
fin_total  <- as.numeric(fin$total_rate[1])
fin_falls  <- as.numeric(fin$fall_rate[1])
pro_total  <- as.numeric(pro$total_rate[1])
pro_falls  <- as.numeric(pro$fall_rate[1])

fatal_df <- as.data.frame(read_excel("fatal.xlsx", col_names = FALSE))
mfg_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[143, 3])
fin_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[453, 3])
pro_fatal_total <- as.numeric(fatal_df[488, 3])
```

## Data Source 1: SOII Table R8 — Nonfatal Injuries by Industry

The Survey of Occupational Injuries and Illnesses (SOII) is published annually by the BLS, as required by the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 [@bls_soii_tables]. It surveys approximately 200,000 employers and reports injury rates per 10,000 full-time equivalent (FTE) workers. The most recent release covers 2023–2024 [@bls_soii_2024].

For this report, we used Table R8, which breaks down nonfatal injury rates by industry and event type (including falls, slips, and trips). We focused on Days Away From Work (DAFW) rates, which capture injuries serious enough to keep workers out of work.

*What it contains:* Annualized nonfatal injury and illness rates per 10,000
full-time equivalent (FTE) workers, broken down by industry (NAICS code) and
event type. Key event types include falls/slips/trips, contact incidents,
overexertion, and transportation incidents. Three case types are reported per
industry: DAFW (Days Away From Work), DJTR (Days of Job Transfer or
Restriction), and DART (the combined measure) [@bls_soii_2024].

*What we use:* Total DAFW rates and fall-specific DAFW rates for manufacturing
(NAICS 31–33), finance and insurance (NAICS 52), and professional services
(NAICS 54). This forms the basis of the primary analysis.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Known Limitation
The SOII relies on employer self-reporting via the OSHA 300 Log. Injuries may
be undercounted if workers face pressure not to report based on severity or other reasons. This could also be attributed to weak record-keeping practices. This limitation applies across all sectors.
:::


## Data Source 2: CFOI Table A-1 — Fatal Injuries by Industry

The Census of Fatal Occupational Injuries (CFOI) is an annual count of every workplace death in the United States [@bls_cfoi_tables]. We used Table A-1, which reports fatal injury counts by industry and event type, including falls.

**What it contains:** A complete census of all fatal workplace injuries in the
U.S. in 2024, broken down by industry (NAICS code) and event type, including
falls, slips, and trips. Unlike the SOII, which is a sample survey, the CFOI
verifies every case using at least two independent source documents: death
certificates, OSHA reports, workers' compensation records, and news reports
[@bls_cfoi_2024].

**What we use:** Total fatal injury counts and fatal fall counts for
manufacturing, finance and insurance, and professional services. This forms the
basis of the secondary/severity analysis.

::: {.callout-note}
## SOII vs CFOI: Key Difference
The SOII is a sample survey: it estimates injury rates from a sample of
employers. The CFOI is a census: it counts every single fatal workplace
injury and verifies it independently. This makes the CFOI more definitive for
fatal outcomes, but it also means the two sources are not directly comparable.
:::


## Data Variables

All variables used for this analysis are documented below. Supresses values are indicative that there are not enough cases for a value to be recorded.

| Variable | Source | Description | Units | Missing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `total_rate` | R8 | Total DAFW injury rate | Per 10,000 FTE workers | — (suppressed if too few cases) |
| `fall_rate` | R8 | Falls, slips, trips DAFW rate | Per 10,000 FTE workers | — (suppressed if too few cases) |
| `fall_share` | Calculated | Falls as % of total nonfatal injuries | Percent | — |
| `fatal_total` | CFOI | Total fatal injuries | Count | — (suppressed if two few cases) |
| `fatal_falls` | CFOI | Fatal falls, slips, trips | Count | — (suppressed if too few cases) |
| `fatal_fall_pct` | Calculated | Fatal falls as % of total deaths | Percent | — |
| `naics` | R8 | Industry classification code | NAICS code | — |
| `case_type` | R8 | DAFW, DJTR, or DART | Categorical | — |
